topic: A person learns their childhood bully became a therapist
raw_output:
[story 1]

The mahogany door clicked shut with an unnerving finality. Elias stood frozen in the hallway, his breath catching in his throat. Across the room, Dr. Thorne—once the architect of his misery, the one who’d orchestrated every humiliating moment of his adolescence—now sat behind a desk bathed in the soft glow of a reading lamp. Thorne wore a tailored suit, his posture impeccable, his expression one of calm, almost serene empathy. Elias’s palms were slick against the worn fabric of his jacket. He wanted to bolt, to melt into the wallpaper, but his feet felt cemented to the polished floor. When Thorne finally looked up, his eyes, once sharp with malice, held a gentle, disconcerting warmth. "Come in, Elias," Thorne said, his voice a low, measured cadence that felt both familiar and utterly alien. The sound vibrated in Elias’s chest, making him instinctively take a half-step backward.

[story 2]

I kept my gaze fixed on the knot in the expensive tie. Every muscle in my jaw was tight, a rigid band refusing to relax. The air in the consultation room was thick, heavy with the scent of sandalwood and old paper. He was there, sitting opposite me, the person who used to make my stomach clench into a tight knot of dread. But now, he was listening. He tilted his head slightly, and the gesture was so subtly reminiscent of the way he used to mock my clumsy attempts at sports. A tremor ran through my hands, causing the pen in my grip to shake violently. "Tell me," he prompted, his tone utterly devoid of judgment, "what weighs on you today?" The question hung in the silence, enormous and demanding. I swallowed hard, the dryness in my mouth making the action feel monumental.

[story 3]

The fluorescent lights hummed overhead, casting harsh shadows that danced across the sterile white walls. I watched him from the doorway, unable to move, rooted to the spot by a profound internal tension. He was gesturing with an open hand, his movements fluid and controlled, discussing trauma with a practiced ease that felt deeply unsettling. It was him. The boy who used to thrive on my discomfort was now dissecting it, packaging it into neat, manageable concepts. A cold wave washed over me, chilling the skin on my arms. I could feel the heat rising in my cheeks, a flush of intense vulnerability I desperately tried to mask by staring intently at a scuff mark on the floor. When he paused, meeting my gaze, there was no mockery there, only an unnerving level of understanding.
